executiveSummary:
**Medicaid Program Performance Summary - January 2026**

National Medicaid enrollment stands at 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with most major states experiencing enrollment declines following the end of pandemic-era continuous coverage protections. California leads enrollment at 12.6 million despite a significant 6.2% year-over-year decrease, while Texas maintains the third-largest program with 4.0 million enrollees and a more moderate 3.1% decline. Notable exceptions to the downward trend include North Carolina, which posted a 0.5% enrollment increase. Total national Medicaid expenditures reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with per-enrollee costs averaging $2,869 nationally. Spending variation across states remains substantial, ranging from Florida's efficient $2,031 per enrollee to Pennsylvania's $4,377 per enrollee, likely reflecting differences in benefit design, provider payment rates, and population health needs.

Quality performance data from 2024 reveals persistent disparities across state programs, with national scores averaging 65.6 out of 100 points. High-performing states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80) significantly outpace struggling programs in the Southeast, where Mississippi (48) and Alabama (52) anchor the bottom rankings. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a quality score of 62, placing it in the moderate performer category but below the national average. The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% exceeds the national average of 83.2%, suggesting strong care coordination infrastructure that could support quality improvement initiatives. Recent federal regulatory activity indicates heightened focus on home and community-based services quality measures for 2028, presenting an opportunity for Texas to align improvement efforts with emerging CMS priorities and potentially advance from its current middle-tier quality standing.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a significant but constrained safety net in America's second-most populous state. The program's enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, closely mirroring the national unwinding trend and performing better than large-state peers like California (-6.2%) and Illinois (-6.2%), though lagging behind North Carolina's modest growth (+0.5%). As a non-expansion state, Texas covers a narrower population than expansion peers, with its enrollment representing roughly 14% of the state's population compared to higher coverage rates in expansion states. The program's $12.0 billion in total expenditures reflects its substantial scale, though the $2,988 per-enrollee spending sits just above the national average of $2,869, indicating relatively efficient cost management compared to higher-spending states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Ohio ($3,561).

Texas demonstrates operational strengths in managed care implementation, with 93.3% penetration rate significantly exceeding the national average of 83.2%, suggesting sophisticated care coordination infrastructure. However, quality performance remains a critical challenge, with Texas scoring 62/100 and ranking 30th nationally—well below high-performing states but avoiding the bottom tier occupied by several southern peers like Arkansas (54) and Alabama (52). This moderate quality performance, combined with the state's non-expansion status, creates a policy tension between cost efficiency and coverage breadth that bears close monitoring. Key areas to watch include potential federal policy changes affecting non-expansion states, the sustainability of managed care quality improvements, and whether Texas can leverage its strong managed care penetration to drive meaningful quality gains while maintaining its relatively controlled per-enrollee costs.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 significantly exceeds the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: High Spending States Show Poor Quality Performance
alerts.description: Pennsylvania and Ohio have high per-enrollee costs ($4,377 and $3,561 respectively) but Ohio lacks corresponding quality data visibility.

alerts.title: Southern States Cluster in Bottom Quality Performers
alerts.description: Four of the five lowest-performing states for quality measures are in the South (AR, SC, AL, MS), suggesting regional quality challenges.

alerts.title: Florida Achieves Lowest Per-Enrollee Spending
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average, representing a potential best practice model.

alerts.title: Massachusetts Leads in Both Quality and High-Value Care
alerts.description: Massachusetts achieves the highest quality score (81) while maintaining reasonable per-enrollee costs at $4,201.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score below national avg - potential contract risk
executiveInsights.summary: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a 62/100 quality score, significantly below the 65.6 national average. This places Texas in the bottom 40% of states for Medicaid quality performance.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Poor quality scores can trigger federal oversight, contract penalties, or reduced federal funding. This directly threatens our contract performance metrics and renewal prospects.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement as urgent priority to HHSC leadership

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declining faster than most comparable states
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY to 4.03M, matching PA's decline rate. Only MI (-7.6%) and IL (-6.2%) among top 10 states declined more sharply.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines reduce contract revenue and may signal operational issues in eligibility processing or member retention that need immediate attention.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment processing workflows for improvement opportunities

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs above national average by $119
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869. This represents a 4.1% premium over national benchmark.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs may indicate inefficiencies in care delivery or population health management that could trigger state budget concerns and contract scrutiny.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost drivers and benchmark against efficient states like FL ($2,031)

executiveInsights.title: New HCBS quality measures for 2028 published by CMS
executiveInsights.summary: CMS published new Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set for 2028 implementation. This represents upcoming regulatory requirements for state Medicaid programs.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures will require system updates, reporting changes, and potentially impact our contract deliverables and performance metrics starting in 2028.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review new HCBS requirements against current contract obligations

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration 10% above national average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas achieves 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M MC enrollees, significantly above the 83.2% national average. This represents strong managed care adoption.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration indicates successful program implementation and provides leverage for value-based care initiatives and cost management strategies.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Leverage high MC penetration data in upcoming contract discussions

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropping 3.1% YoY could accelerate, reducing program scale and contract values.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop retention analytics and redetermination support capabilities to help HHSC stabilize enrollment.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality score improvement
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 with 62/100 quality score, creating opportunity for performance-based contract expansion.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose comprehensive quality improvement initiative targeting top 5-10 measures with highest impact.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration signals potential major procurement cycle for MCO oversight systems.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor HHSC procurement portal and prepare managed care modernization proposal.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight exposure
riskOpportunity.description: Active OIG Medicaid oversight increases compliance risk but creates opportunity for audit readiness services.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish dedicated OIG compliance monitoring and response capability for Texas HHSC.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality measures
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 HCBS quality measure requirements create implementation opportunity and compliance complexity.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop HCBS quality measurement platform proposal for Texas HHSC LTSS programs.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas $2,988 per-enrollee cost above national average creates budget pressure and efficiency demands.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose cost containment analytics platform focusing on high-cost member identification and intervention.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive displacement risk
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple states showing procurement activity increases risk of competitor wins affecting Texas positioning.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen Texas HHSC relationship through proactive innovation showcases and executive engagement.

riskOpportunity.label: Data collection modernization
riskOpportunity.description: New CMS generic information collection requirements create opportunity for data platform modernization contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive data modernization proposal addressing new CMS reporting requirements.
